PAKISTAN
W
AR = 2 / HKC = 0
In the last 6 months, the state of Pakistan has managed to enter into its 2nd armed conflicts with its
Truce hopes. neighbors on both side, yet in even more time (8.5 months and counting just this year) and ever
since the Pakistani government announced the adoption of the HKC, not a single yard has
managed to attain its accreditation as yet. And while the Pakistani Air Force dropped bombs on
alleged Taliban terror camps in Afghanistan, the real progress that the rest of the business world
is waiting for, remains dreadfully out of site.
While provisional DASR approvals have kept local deliveries primed for survival until yards tidy

time (8.5 months and counting just this year) and ever
since the Pakistani government announced the adoption of the HKC, not a single yard has
managed to attain its accreditation as yet. And while the Pakistani Air Force dropped bombs on
alleged Taliban terror camps in Afghanistan, the real progress that the rest of the business world
is waiting for, remains dreadfully out of site.
While provisional DASR approvals have kept local deliveries primed for survival until yards tidy
and gear up to be fully accredited, the absence of news pertaining to potential international class
authorities visiting Pakistan to confirm progress / issue certifications remain suspiciously amiss -
granted not too many in the industry would be keen to visit an active war zone.
Global turmoil.
And as global markets continue to react negatively to Trump's chain reaction of ill-advised
economic moves that are once again ratcheting up tariff threats against China (reportedly another
100% hike) and USTR (amongst another delayed U.S. tariffs) that come into effect, are leading to a
host of Chinese reciprocal measures that stand to throw the shipping industry into disarray, should
these tariffs come to pass. Pakistan's 30% tariff rate has also done little to ease the hot plastic
summer slide that's been burning through Pakistan's inflation rate ever since they were announced
back in August, with inflation nearly doubled over the last month.
Adding to the spiciness of the present is the perennial problem of trying to snag a juicy large LDT
Sp icy. dry unit, which remains far from the interests of respective owners or cash buyers who are wary
of moving large units to an HKC uncertain Pakistan. Moreover, with an utter lack of local arrivals
(not a single fresh unit arrived this week and the only one at anchorage is a problem child from
September), domestic steel mills have reportedly resorted to sourcing competing steel product
from Iran resulting in the otherwise rock steady local steel plate price tumbling about USD 3/Ton
and down to USD 616.80/Ton by the time the week ended. The PKR wasn't enjoying much of a
"Prom Queen" moment either, losing about 18 basis points against the U.S. Dollar as the week
ended and closed it out at PKR 283.20)
Unce rtainty index. Overall, Pakistan's uncertainty index seems shakier than Bangladesh's and this remains clearly

TURKEY
J
UST KEEP GOING!
Like the jingle from "Finding Nemo", the Lira "just keeps going" down and down as it lost another
Strugglingly and
30 basis points against the U.S. Dollar and slumped even lower to end the week at a disastrous and
ironic.
record-breaking TRY 41.82. And while this has still not been the "INR 90" moment for the Lira (given
that local recyclers are expecting to see levels cross TRY 50), the proportionate drop in local offerings
in comparison to those from India, remain far more stable.
Yet, at what cost? When tonnage during these weakening financial times is certainly heading towards
sub-continent shores vs. engaging with EUSRR rules and regulations. Struggling, yet ironic times
indeed.

Global markets continue in turmoil leaving the shipping industry in a true state of disarray as one segment of shipping has remainedalarmingly / surprisingly busy over the last few years while the other opposite end of the segment has been dropped off to an empty pool. Trading markets continue to firm as the Baltic Shipping Dry Index inched up just over 1% this week, backed by the perennial firming of capes, panamax and dry indices, all of which reported gains of 2.1%, 1%, and 2% respectively.
And as charter rates climb and the industry continues to raise the bar of the overall age group of the world's floating sector, oil decidedly continued its Lira-like descent into the high USD 50s/barrel as it clocked the week out at USD 57.38 / barrel - a whopping 8% decline over the last month alone and an 18% drop since the same time last year.
As cheaper oil and firmer rates help ship owners net wider margins, the ship recycling end of the industry (the empty pool end) has been and continues being deprived of tonnage as year after year, it has been the same story post-covid i.e. next year. And 4+ years into it, things have only gotten worse now that prices seem intent to slip back into the high-USD 300's/ton, especially in India where an alarming few weeks have passed with Rupee drops, steel declines, and sentiments drowning.
In fact, as we zoom out to the macro of the micro, the overall Indian sub-continent ship recycling market seems intent on withdrawing their levels at the bidding tables this week as even Bangladesh and Pakistan boarded India's financial roller-coaster, seemingly benchmarking themselves at lower (than their last) levels, all while an air of uncertainty seems ready to gift wrap the upcoming season even though a sudden spike in incoming tonnage over recent weeks at the various anchorages would seem to paint a rather different picture than what is actually playing out on the ground.
Finally, while the conflict in Gaza appears to have actually set a ceasefire rolling, it remains to be seen just how smoothly Red Sea routes open up again with Houthi activity having gone quiet (not silenced) for the time being. And while a resumption of traffic could certainly see global prices start to decline over the long run, for the short term, the impact on the supply of recycling units remains uncertain at this juncture, especially as

than what is actually playing out on the ground.
Finally, while the conflict in Gaza appears to have actually set a ceasefire rolling, it remains to be seen just how smoothly Red Sea routes open up again with Houthi activity having gone quiet (not silenced) for the time being. And while a resumption of traffic could certainly see global prices start to decline over the long run, for the short term, the impact on the supply of recycling units remains uncertain at this juncture, especially as prices are about break past the USD 200/Ton price-gram from the highs of just 22 months ago.
When that day comes, hopefully we won't see a deluge of tonnage being proposed for recycling all at once, but rather a staggered, manageable influx of vessels proposed to allow the industry the dignity to digest tonnage in line with the recently implemented Hong Kong Convention. Prices will logically take a hit as supply increases and that will be exacerbated with a growing shadow / dark fleet, as reportedly about 700 vessels have gone ‘dark' of late - just like Turkey's performance.

BANGLADESH
I
NDELIBLY INDECISIVE.
Over the last couple of weeks, after a seemingly unexpected "upturn" (if you can even call it one)
Un expected boon.
in the fluctuating intentions of the Bangladeshi market saw a couple of decent priced sales finally
take place to hungry local recyclers, including a cape and an MR sized tanker from existing cash
buyer inventories. However, by the time the week ended, Chattogram was back in a slump with
the few open buyers having booked tonnage and the local market seemingly retreating back to
being indecisive - an indelible signature of their 2025 performance.
And this week of course, was no different. Even though Bangladesh was the only sub-continent
ship recycling destination to report no declines to their local steel plate prices, they remain stranded
at USD 519/Ton with ongoing news of recycled steel inventories just stockpiling at domestic yards,
Timed out.
which justifies the sporadic extent of purchases from local ship recyclers. And the distaste is in the
pudding as other than oldies idling for weeks on end at local anchorage, not a single new vessel
has arrived Chattogram this week.
The Taka meanwhile delivered the brunt of the market hit this week as it dell nearly 0.5% and
clocked the week out at over BDT 122 against the U.S. Dollar once again, leaving local recyclers
with expensive purchases, unsold inventory, and no yard space all blended into a curdled milk
shake as this market desperately awaits a new government to spearhead the direction of the nation,
Infl ation station. which is pulling up to inflation station once again.

Overall, even though the last couple of quarters had seen some constant price declined and reduced
demand HKC yard upgrades came front and center whilst local authorities themselves scrambled
to layout the new requirements, documentary procedures, and standards to be kept, the market
has found itself without a synchromesh gearbox. With many yards lying dormant and LC / bank
limits left to satisfy, certain end buyers have seen it fit to pick up some of the admittedly meagre
supply of vessels at some of the best (spelled lowest) rates seen in the sub-continent for some
months.
We can only hope some of this purchasing activity continues until February 2026 and this nation
Forefront/ returns to the forefront of the buying, despite having only (likely) having 21 approved yards by
that time.

green HKC units intended for an India only redelivery are now having to suffer as a result of the lack
of participation from the local recycling community.

PAKISTAN
R
UNNING TO STAND STILL!
Pakistan is the one market likely to race to the bottom of rankings before the year's end with the
Iranian steel woes. latest batch of hurdles coming their way across October.
Inflation has nearly doubled in September, the PKR has lost 38 basis points against the U.S. Dollar
just this week at PKR 283.62 and cheaper incoming Iranian steel / raw material has been realigning
the focus of domestic steel mills away from the local ship recycling community for 2 weeks leaving
local steel plate prices declining USD 5/Ton this week, down to USD 614/Ton - a grand total trip of
Buyers ready to acquire USD 15/Ton in just over 2 weeks.
Ensuing local offerings along with sentiments and demand have understandably suffered, leaving
end buyers spooked and largely resuming their coveted wait-and-watch mode when tabling offers
Some yards booked.
on the few pieces of tonnage making the rounds basis a potential Pakistan re-delivery, which have
remained amiss from the most part.
Notwithstanding, several dry bulk units were reportedly committed basis a Pakistan redelivery in
the USD 420s/LDT (and above) over recent weeks and it seemed as though Pakistan was back in
spades, especially given that a 22K LDT tanker and a 9K LDT bulker arrived locally just this week,
leaving Gadani recyclers with nearly 33K LDT of tonnage via 3 ships.
But this week's setbacks aren't the only ones leaving the local ship recycling community with an
overdose of pain killers as their HKC nightmare has not even started, let alone any accreditations
HK C focus. being granted to the first yard. This is clearly in the back of ship owners and cash buyers' minds
given the heat around sanctioned vessels of late and the possibility of now running afoul of HKC
regulations in a post HKC world.
As such, the current situation has once again left this this market marginally above a woeful India
and it is likely to slip past the Alang market, which itself seems to be in freefall at present.

TURKEY

URREAL RIDE!
Turkey's slippage has been the hallmark of this market's performance for 2 years now and it
Not nice.
continues performing unrestrictedly in the worst way imaginable. Lira is down another 35 basis

 Animals process time differently depending on their size. Research suggests that smaller animals like mice and lizards

fade-resistant enamel.

MARKET SUMMARY
INDIA
The market continues to remain subdued, with little to no positive momentum. Activity
levels are low, and overall sentiment stays weak amid persistently poor fundamentals.
Although the exchange rate has shown some improvement, it has not yet translated
into firmer vessel prices, which continue to stagnate.
A few recyclers who had purchased vessels at lower acquisition levels are now able to
offer materials at reduced selling prices, putting additional strain on others operating
at normal cost levels.
BANGLADESH
The market in Bangladesh has softened further, and this downward trend may become
more evident in the pricing of the coming week. Local scrap prices remain at low levels,
with very little movement seen across categories.
Despite the weak sentiment, buyer interest persists, primarily due to the limited
availability of tonnage in the market. Buyers continue to show stronger inclination
toward tankers and bulkers, which remain their preferred options under current
conditions.
PAKISTAN
The market remains largely stagnant with a slightly negative tone as vessel prices
continue to decline. The full impact of this downward trend may become more visible
in the coming weeks.
Demand is quite limited, with only two or three active buyers currently capable of
making purchases.
Market participants are also closely watching the foreign exchange situation, which is
expected to influence overall stability and buying interest in the days ahead.

[Page 3]
TURKIYE
The Turkish market has shown slight improvement this week, with local scrap prices
rising by around USD 3. Import levels remain stable, and vessel prices have also
recorded a modest increase.
This marks a gradual strengthening in overall market sentiment after a relatively quiet
period.

Global markets seemed to take a breather this week as things turned slow across the board once again, not only on the shipping side but even the ship recycling side of things. Currencies fluctuated in moderate tandem, local steel plate prices made surprisingly marginal moves, all while the Baltic Exchange Dry Index slipped and oil steadied itself. The BDI reported a declining week that shaved off about 3.2% by CoB Friday, dropping to its lowest levels since early October as the Cape index took the lead shaving off over 6% of its value, while Supramaxes dipped a comparatively milder 9 points and the Panamax index remained steady for the week even though the overall index is nearly 4% down.
As inflation rose marginally in the U.S. (climbing to 3% by the end of the September), oil managed to gain some traction this week, firming to USD 62.14/barrel - a 1% climb on the back of anticipations on a possible China - U.S. trade deal, all while Trump threatens Putin with further escalations as sweeping sanctions take effect, not only on Russian oil trade (particularly with India), but even the EU now hopping on the wagon, reportedly blacklisting hundreds of container vessels this week.
And as the global dark / shadow fleets start to grow, the woeful performance of the Indian sub-continent ship recycling markets continues on and there are few emerging signs of light at the end of the tunnel (more like an oncoming freight train) for the remainder of this year. Prices have fallen so low over recent weeks that constant discussions of prices below USD 400/LDT are comfortably prevalent and it seems increasingly certain that any viable recovery has still not bought a ticket to the sub-continent recycling sector.
India has endured the roughest times over the last several weeks with levels plummeting on the back of rapidly deteriorating local steel prices and a drastically depreciating currency that continues to suffer on the back of 50% tariffs amidst the U.S. - India - Russia love triangle. Pakistan has not been too far behind the recent falls with plunging demand due to an influx of cheaper steel imports, all while Bangladesh shows moderate signs of life with a couple of local arrivals. The occasional and aggressive offerings on larger LDT units have been so sporadic, it has left ship owners and cash buyers confused as to where levels on the ground really stand. And as India celebrates a quieter time thanks to Di

of 50% tariffs amidst the U.S. - India - Russia love triangle. Pakistan has not been too far behind the recent falls with plunging demand due to an influx of cheaper steel imports, all while Bangladesh shows moderate signs of life with a couple of local arrivals. The occasional and aggressive offerings on larger LDT units have been so sporadic, it has left ship owners and cash buyers confused as to where levels on the ground really stand. And as India celebrates a quieter time thanks to Diwali Holidays, Turkey remains seemingly absent regardless of work or play.
Overall, mounting global trade wars and tariff implementations are leaving global corporations confused and fearing the worst, should these reciprocal and seemingly ever escalatory measures continue to unfold thereby stifling shipping sectors, global trade, FX markets, steel products, and overall cost of living. For the time being, freight rates remain relatively firm, and the suffocating inflow of recycling tonnage has remained painfully thin, as we ease into the November 2025 and have little to show for yet another year of record low supply.

EPRESSINGLY SELECTIVE!
As the Chattogram ship recycling sector continues trying to balance itself on stilts in the middle of
Loo king for large.
an earthquake, surprisingly decent levels have been tabled from some of the hungry / premium
buyers on some of the select large(r) LDT vessels proposed into Bangladesh - capesize bulkers and
Membrane type LNG carriers including some of the recent sales (mentioned below). However, the
supply of those units has been transient traffic with smaller LDT vessels making up the bulk of the
inward recycling rotation and is now seeing almost no interest from local buyers.
As the economic situation remains confounded with inflation rising marginally last month, 30%
tariffs on the nation's most vital exports (Readymade Garments), the Taka losing nearly 30 basis
points against the Dollar this week (closing it out at BDT 122.35), and a local steel plate price that
Beacon of hope.
made a surprise opening at levels far higher than last week's closing, but still declining about USD
3/Ton is certainly a sign of impending times for this market.
All eyes are therefore glued to the upcoming elections that have been tentatively scheduled for
February 2026 and loom like a beacon of hope (or an axe swinging on a rope) as locally, the drive
to oust an ineffective interim regime that has failed to stimulate the economy via any new
infrastructure / development projects since June 2024, is being attributed as the key reason behind
the nation's economic decline. Notwithstanding, HKC yard upgrades have at least made decent

On the sales front, the recycling LNG clear-out continued this week as after an agonizing wait, it
has been confirmed that two 1995 built Membrane units PUTERI NILAM (26,915 LDT) and PUTERI
DELIMA (26,915) have been committed on an enbloc basis to a buyer on private terms. One other
sale was confirmed this week as the handymax bulk carrier from Waruna MONICA P (7,779 LDT)
was fixed for a decent USD 380/LT LDT basis an ‘as is' Belawan delivery, earmarking in the only
sale of the week with a likely intended resale into Bangladesh.

And despite all 100+ yards in India being HKC certified even before the HKC came into force, market
driven scarcity of viable tonnage that has been stalking the ship recycling industry for years now, has Market struggles.
left the Alang buyers with no real tonnage to work on as prices fade from the highs of USD 600/Ton
down to below USD 400/Ton once again, leaving them at a greater disadvantage (as compared to the
neighbouring competition) in terms of putting their HKC accreditations to use whilst competing
markets are still getting up to speed.
Part of the problem has been the supply of dark / shadow vessels into India and the willingness of
certain end buyers to acquire these units cheaply despite the international regulations and sanctions
being in place), meaning there is now a two-tiered market that is killing prices for clean vessels being
pr oposed. Shadow shame.

basis points against the U.S. Dollar and end the week at INR 87.54 while local steel plate prices waddled
out of the bar at sunrise with a USD 1/Ton improvement, yet leaning against the pillars of USD 389/Ton

PAKISTAN
P
AYING THE PRICE!
Just as the Gadani market had seemingly gotten back on its feet over the last couple of weeks with
Cheap steel influx. fresh tonnage incoming on the back of the highest ongoing offerings in the Indian sub-continent
ship recycling sector with some decent LDT dry tonnage palliating the local appetite, a sudden and
recent influx of cheap Iranian scrap steel has local offerings and prevailing sentiments nosediving
into the abyss this week.
Whilst not quite as low as the miserable state of the Indian market at present, Pakistani ship
Buyers ready to acquire recyclers are becoming feverishly concerned by this recent undercutting of their local inventories
and have refr

the highest ongoing offerings in the Indian sub-continent
ship recycling sector with some decent LDT dry tonnage palliating the local appetite, a sudden and
recent influx of cheap Iranian scrap steel has local offerings and prevailing sentiments nosediving
into the abyss this week.
Whilst not quite as low as the miserable state of the Indian market at present, Pakistani ship
Buyers ready to acquire recyclers are becoming feverishly concerned by this recent undercutting of their local inventories
and have refrained from tabling offers on any of the slim pickings of vessels that maybe making
the rounds.
Refrain.
As it stands, Gadani is still positioned just above USD 400/LDT and as is the case with their
Bangladeshi counterparts, Pakistani ship recyclers are looking mostly for larger LDT units
(specifically dry vessels) if they are able to get their hands on any. This change in in the local flavor
is primarily due to the difficulty and time needed to get provisional DASR certifications from the
relevant authorities whilst HKC yards are still ongoing and upgrades taking place, thereby
rendering small LDT units increasingly less favorable (for the effort needed to import them and
the shorter times to recycle them).
Fundamentals too have been chocking as not only has inflation nearly doubled across September
on the back of U.S. sanctions and a fresh new war with the Afghani Taliban, but the Pakistan Rupee
Ab ove 400, just. doubled back 20 basis points this week just like their Indian counterparts and closed the week out
at PKR 283.50. Local steel plate prices also resumed flatlining at USD 614/Ton, after falling over
USD 15/Ton over recent weeks.
This is only adding to local frustrations as their ability to compete is being hampered by geography
and the opening of Bangladesh viable tonnage of late, leaving Pakistan as the market to keep an
eye out, especially if you have a dry unit opening in the area and a Gadani buyer gets up to grab a
bite.

improvement after a prolonged quiet period.
However, the U.S. dollar has strengthened considerably, limiting any positive impact
this minor shift could have brought.
Overall, the market remains flat, with sentiment still cautious and activity levels
subdued despite this faint hint of optimism.
BANGLADESH
The market remains largely subtle, with limited movement observed over the week.
Overall activity levels remain moderate.
Apart from an isolated transaction involving the sale of a large-sized tanker at
unusually high levels, considered unsustainable under current conditions, sentiment
remains steady without major change in pricing or buyer interest.
Yards in Bangladesh have started receiving DASR certification, with more expected to
follow in the coming period.
PAKISTAN
The Pakistani market continues to struggle amid very weak demand and declining
activity levels.
Prices for shredded steel have fallen further, adding to the overall market pressure.
In addition, illegal imports from Iran are disrupting local market dynamics, further
weakening sentiment and limiting any prospects of short-term recovery.

A spooky end to October delivered its share of unending surprises not only across the ship recycling arena, but even global markets at large.At the onset, Trump wraps up his Southeast Asian glamour tour touting deals made, especially one with China where an embargo on retaliatory trade sanctions and set offs between the two nations was set for at least a year, bringing a sigh of relief to embattled vessels enroute to discharge in Chinese waters and even global consumers at large. With September inflationary numbers on the rise and U.S. figures being released only a couple of weeks back (due to the ongoing government shutdown), November seems on track to deliver another set of surprises for ship recycling markets. Oil meanwhile slipped nearly 1% during the week as it lingered around USD 60.67, with OPEC+ announcing further cutbacks in the supply of oil in upcoming Q1 2026 (adieu 2025), leaving lingering expectations of increasing gas prices.
Supply side was no different as the Baltic Exchange's Dry Index also reported a noteworthy fall as the overall index fell nearly 1.3% for the week (on the back of all sub-segments reporting losses), down nearly 8% for the month of October and further marking its first monthly decline since April 2025. And this slowdown is increasingly presenting itself via tonnages of various sizes and types across the Indian sub-continent ship recycling landscape, especially in India where despite the lowest prices in the region, Alang continues to report an eye-popping number of occasional arrivals. And yet, the glut of tonnage sailing the high seas remains disproportionately ratioed against the number of units recycled over the last several years, leaving a potential surge of incoming tonnage on the horizon.
As a result, the ship recycling industry has continued to endure a stultifying and sluggish Q4 (in line with the numerous quarters spanning the last few years) with declines evident across all sectors and sales (barring the occasional stunners) occurring at ever deteriorating levels. An eye-catching duo of LNGs sold last week (as a clear-out from this beleaguered sector continues) were just such a highlight, including a seemingly steady trickle of older dissolute handymax sized bulk carriers sold in recent weeks as well (as confirmed by the various port reports). Sales of sanctioned vessels have also shaken the industry with fixtures being reported at well below market pricing into a dithering Alang, establishing a two-tiered price system

) occurring at ever deteriorating levels. An eye-catching duo of LNGs sold last week (as a clear-out from this beleaguered sector continues) were just such a highlight, including a seemingly steady trickle of older dissolute handymax sized bulk carriers sold in recent weeks as well (as confirmed by the various port reports). Sales of sanctioned vessels have also shaken the industry with fixtures being reported at well below market pricing into a dithering Alang, establishing a two-tiered price system that is causing confusion and consternation in the industry.
Overall, despite a reported end to the U.S.-Chinese trade tit-for-tat, the constant volatility and ever-changing headline decisions on tariffs and sanctions are now being treated with assured suspicion and careful wariness by international businesses and MNCs. As such, the final months of the year will wind up another haunting 202X for recycling locations, marked by declining levels, starved supply, regulatory red tape, HKC troubles, immovable recycled product, political unrest, tariffs, oil sanctions, and a whole lot more. Someone call Hollywood and make a movie already.

who have miraculously managed to retain 1st place on the market rankings for a healthy portion

competition on that side of the seaways, Pakistani recyclers have to compete with India, which is

coming into force, the focus to align all HKC yard upgrades towards fruition remains amiss as not

This is causing consternation for those owners and cash buyers who are considering options on a

and authorities aren't entirely grounded on the new format of arrival procedures and are thereby
facing hiccups. Case and point, the single straggler at anchorage that's been there since late
No go low LDT. September despite mediocre arrivals deliveries ongoing into his market - including this week.
Notwithstanding, provisional DASR certificates have at least ensured some of the incoming
tonnage is going through its share of evaluation and safer recycling practices. But how long will
this continue with plate prices flatlining and the PKR itself hitting 283.17 against the U.S. Dollar
while Iranian steel pulls the rug from underneath local steel plate prices. It seems as though until
HKC accreditations come firmly into play, this destination will likely fester at the top spot in
isolation. And not because they are able to offer the highest, but because competing markets
continue to slide.

TURKEY
S
UPPLY PINCH!
As the utter lack of supply continues to hammer Aliaga for several years now thanks to the red tape
4 years.
of bureaucracy and coupled with a near absence of Turkey-only market tonnage for nearly 4 years
now, it wasn't unexpected to see local recyclers raise their offers in order to tempt owners who may
only be in the deliberation stages of recycling their units at this time.
But fundamentals remain far from their side as although local steel plate prices have made marginal
improvements, the TRY (ending the week at 42.06 against the U.S.$) has done more damage to keep
local business operating at usual speeds.
And while inflation (at a whopping +33%) may have countered some of the resale potential of
recycled ship's steel, it's a matter of time until Turkey's government makes appropriate fiscal changes
to balance the cost of living, while simultaneously squeezing the pace of the domestic ship recycling
sector, especially as lending rates remain unattainable for tier-B (and below) recyclers.

MARKET SUMMARY
INDIA
The Indian market showed some recovery last week, with renewed buying interest
offering a sense of improvement.
However, with Bangladesh continuing to pay notably higher prices, most sellers remain
inclined to divert tonnage there, leaving India with limited activity.
The arrival of sanctioned vessels at substantially lower levels could challenge the
competitiveness of other local yards in the near term.
BANGLADESH
The Bangladeshi market has softened slightly, though it continues to attract firm buyer
interest, especially for tankers, where sentiment remains relatively positive.
In contrast, bulk carrier prices have weakened further, reflecting a more cautious
approach among recyclers.
Despite the softer tone, Bangladesh retains a clear lead over regional competitors,
maintaining stronger price levels and consistent momentum.
PAKISTAN
The Pakistani market remains largely unchanged, with dull sentiment and limited
activity persisting for another week.
However, the government's plan to establish a new green ship recycling yard at Port
Qasim has drawn industry attention, as it could bring much-needed modernization,
environmental compliance, and renewed investor confidence to the sector in the
longer term.

[Page 3]
TURKIYE
Market sentiment and price levels have remained stable over the past few weeks,
showing no major movement in either direction.
The ongoing shortage of available tonnage has kept activity subdued, as limited supply
continues to restrict new transactions.
The Central Bank of Türkiye has projected year-end inflation at around 31%, indicating
that price pressures in the economy remain elevated.

Some other day, just not today, or this week, or this month, or this year, or the last few for that matter.As president economic turmoil tears down the White House back home and economies around the world, his actions are directly leading to a meltdown that has consistently been affecting international markets month after month, including the advent of November 2025 as inflation is still rising at key ship recycling destinations, global steel prices are collapsing, currencies devaluing like it's an overdue assignment, and a disjointed ratio of trading units vs recycling volumes, all wrapped up in a 2025 burrito of unnecessary bombastic.
To start, inflation is on the rise again in Pakistan with Turkey and Bangladesh reporting barely noticeable corrections - all while India and USA figures are still lampooning with pending updates at their ends. The Baltic Exchange also rose to the tune of nearly 2% in just about a month with Capes, Panamax, and smaller segment indices all clocking the week out with 3.1%, 09%, and 0.5% respective gains and the overall index firming 7% just this week. As sanctions and bans increasingly strike at the world's trading units and the balance of the legally trading group seems increasingly burdened with shouldering the load of missing tonnage, which itself cannot even be recycled anywhere, it is certainly starting to show at the waterfront once again. And for the world of ship recycling, is a disastrous turn of events as 2026 is already at attention on the horizon.
Oil is likely to see similar performance hiccups as it declined nearly 20 basis points to end the week at levels barely above USD 60/barrel thank to global supply being on cue to outpace demand as the U.S. ramps up domestic production and investors continue to monitor the effects of Washington's pressure-tactics on Moscow via pending sanctions on their top oil majors, including Rosneft PJSC and LukOil PJSC (LukOil even rumored to be filing for bankruptcy this week) and purchasing nations such as China and India now facing further economic pressures of their own. And all this while Dollars and lack of diplomacy are steering currencies into neverland.
At the micro end, an increasingly defunct sub-continent ship recycling sector is struggling to attain anywhere near reasonable / expected levels for another week as any vessels that are being proposed, are swiftly greeted with disappointing levels and are subsequently withdrawn to trade another day. Levels in both Pakistan and India have

rumored to be filing for bankruptcy this week) and purchasing nations such as China and India now facing further economic pressures of their own. And all this while Dollars and lack of diplomacy are steering currencies into neverland.
At the micro end, an increasingly defunct sub-continent ship recycling sector is struggling to attain anywhere near reasonable / expected levels for another week as any vessels that are being proposed, are swiftly greeted with disappointing levels and are subsequently withdrawn to trade another day. Levels in both Pakistan and India have sunk firmly below USD 400/LDT as Bangladesh similarly struggles with declining steel prices and a viciously inert demand but still manages to remain just above USD 400/LDT. Apart from the odd LNG and older handymax bulkers with surveys due, there has been very little supply keeping recyclers active. On the flip side, this shortage has perhaps been a blessing in disguise as HKC upgrades continue at pace in both Bangladesh and (reportedly) Pakistan, with 18 yards have so far been HKC approved in Chattogram and 3 more set to follow before the end of the year, all while Gadani has yet to receive its first HKC yard approval as DASR certificate issuances allow local yards to stay afloat. Turkey is still waiting for them to join the party.

No thing viable.
Choppers to have no viable market candidates to work on for yet another week as most owners of
smaller-than-large-ldt units seem far more disappointed with the declined level of local offerings
at the bidding tables and are happier to either engage competing markets or continue trading their
units instead, eking out the final earnings for even overaged vessels lurking in the global fleets,
rather than face the dour state of Bangladesh's ship recycling reality today.
And that even goes for the better placed local open buyers who continue to pay well-above local
market levels just to fill empty plots, utilize dormant L/C limits, and minimize operational and
fixed losses in the interim.
Continue trading.
But given Bangladesh's economic reality? Rightly so, with the Taka having collapsed from a high

after lackluster resales of recycled ships steel onto domestic steel mills and competing cheaper
imports ongoing, local steel plate prices that consistently seemed to defy gravity and remain
suspended at surprising levels, all but fell off a Bangladeshi cliff as the week ended with no trading
reported. Next week will certainly present a clearer picture.

Inflation meanwhile has been see-sawing month after month as it fell to 8.48% in June, rose to 8.55%
in July, back down to 8.29% in August, back up in September to 8.39% and now 8.17% as October
ended. This is tumultuous for any nation's economy as there is no resounding direction for the
country and a clear failure of governance policy, which itself is due for a resurrection in February
2026 as riots and political instability are back in the headlines once again. And after a brief spell of
marginal conclusions, the nation's waterfront is back down to a stranded lone wolf with no fresh
arrivals this week.
As such, it is set to be a much quieter end to 2025 than previously perceived - especially in a giant

market like Ba nglad esh and especially after a promising start to the year, but an uncertain mid-
year HKC + Trump-cola cocktail with an election cherry on top will ensure the end of the year for
the Chattogram Choppers will be a messy one.

strung an entirely different chord for the nation's economy as inflation has skyrocketed from 3%
back in August to 6.2% last month, further adding to Gadani's perennially confusing vessel pricing
strategies.
HKC hurdles meanwhile remain the primary contributors to the afflictions of this market amidst

prosecution outcomes - and a Gadani market that is still twiddling on temporary DASR approvals
just to keep the domestic sector alive as despite news of ongoing local infrastructure upgrades, not
a single yard has received its accreditation, far from officially entering the HKC arena.
No go low LDT. Local deliveries too have dried up with Pakistan and Bangladesh twinning their anchorages as no
fresh arrivals were reported for this week in Gadani as well.
As such, quiet has mostly descended on the waterfront with little for end buyers to chirp about
and like ongoing interactions at the bidding tables, ship owners and cash buyer options are
invariably funneled down to a stable Alang. Go figure!

MARKET SUMMARY
INDIA
The market witnessed a slight pickup at the very end of the week, but overall
conditions remain largely unchanged due to the strengthened U.S. dollar, which
continues to limit market movement.
Buying interest exists but remains restricted because of the ongoing shortage of
tonnage. Whatever interest does emerge is mostly at lower levels.
The continued inflow of dark-fleet/shadow-fleet units at significantly cheaper prices is
putting additional pressure on local markets, as these vessels are being turned around
quickly and pulling overall sentiment down.
The DASR is still pending, and recyclers, as well as other market participants-continue
to press the authorities for its release.
BANGLADESH
Market activity remains quiet with no meaningful change in sentiment. Conditions are
largely the same as in previous weeks.
Buyers remain interested in 8,000-10,000 LDT units, but availability is extremely
limited. Larger vessels are attracting less attention mainly because only one or two
buyers currently have the LC capability to pursue them.
Ongoing LC constraints remain the main hurdle for the market, restricting the ability of
most buyers to commit to new tonnage.
PAKISTAN
The Pakistani market shows no change and continues to lean on the weaker side.
Sentiment remains flat with no signs of improvement.
Buying activity is minimal, and no significant movement in pricing or demand has been
observed.

[Page 3]
TURKIYE
Conditions in Türkiye remain unchanged from the previous week. The market continues
to follow its usual pattern, showing very little movement week-to-week.
Sentiment and activity remain the same as before, with no notable developments or
shifts observed during

With the last 6 weeks of 2025 now firmly on the horizon, the final stretch of the year continues to demonstrateits sheer unwillingness to compromise as global economies are still reporting volatile movements of commodity prices and oil futures that are still unfolding with each passing week. But the ship recycling market economies are the ones generating even more confounding results amid an increasingly precariously posture at just how this momentum has been unravelling over the last 3 - 4 years and is now expected (feared) to likely continue deep into 2026.
While the Baltic Exchange Dry Index reported gains once again this week on the back of virtually all inclusive sub-sectors reporting improvements of their own through the week, oil futures on the other hand seem to be taking a beating after levels clocked the week out at USD 59.50/barrel after Ukraine successfully carried out a massive drone attack against Russia's largest oil refinery located at Novorossiysk. The U.S. Dollar meanwhile continues its carnage across the globe as it firmed at nearly all locations across the board while local steel plate prices in the two steady recycling destinations retreated in varying degrees.
This double gut-punch targeted at the purchasing prowess of the ship recycling fraternity has driven demand and vessel offerings even further down this week as vintage tonnage starts to unexpectedly arrive at anchorages i.e. Bangladesh, which healthily beat India's beaching quota for this week. Moreover, with freight rates continually rising / yielding profitable results for relevant stakeholders, recycling sales have been slow for the most part of the year even though sales activity has comparatively picked up a touch since the barren summer slump. Worse yet, many of the transactions concluded are at levels far below expectations and in the low USD 400s/LDT, with several reportedly in the high USD 300s/LDT on less preferred / small LDT tonnage.
Meanwhile, the poorer placed India and Pakistan continue struggling to register any worthwhile levels for owners / cash buyers despite the supply of older dry bulk units - particularly vintage handymax and panamax units including small -to medium- sized-tankers - registering an increase in the number of candidates being proposed for recycling. Yet, just how many are actually being concluded amidst a sea of boring offers remains inconclusive even though several end-of-life MR tankers, smaller product tankers, and a steady stream of occasional LNGs have kept ship recycling markets from dozing off completely in the latter part of this year.
Finally,

the supply of older dry bulk units - particularly vintage handymax and panamax units including small -to medium- sized-tankers - registering an increase in the number of candidates being proposed for recycling. Yet, just how many are actually being concluded amidst a sea of boring offers remains inconclusive even though several end-of-life MR tankers, smaller product tankers, and a steady stream of occasional LNGs have kept ship recycling markets from dozing off completely in the latter part of this year.
Finally, as HKC developments have taken precedent over filling plots and with mandatory yard upgrades marring the recycling progress, Bangladesh remains with only 18 HKC approved yards while Pakistan is at the tail end of the race as it awaits its first HKC approval. And as the shipping calendar remains busy with various events over the past month or so, including Bahri week last week in addition to the recycling world convening in Hong Kong for the annual Tradewinds Ship Recycling forum, it remains a busy time for the industry - all while Turkey remains alone at the USD 2XX/Ton party awaiting a lone passerby.

for the first in a few quarters. Not to be outdone, the PKR too fell about 11 basis points and closed

This has left Gadani positioned just above a lackluster Alang and leaving not much else on the
table in terms of safe redeliveries. Case-and-point, if a tiny 1400 Ton straggler can manage to
remain stranded at anchorage for nearly 2 months, it certainly leaves no room for owners / cash
buyers of larger LDT units to risk the Pakistani market, especially amidst this ongoing decline.
HKC hurdles have certainly left this market stranded in the face of the competition as no new
vessels have arrived Gadani this week and authorities will ultimately have to fuel progress at
Pr essure needed. yards in order to remain relevant. Even with incoming cheaper steel product, higher tariffs on
imported steel need to be imposed so that the state of affairs doesn't cannibalize local business
viability.
The big question remains, when and how soon will any of this come into effect as it is clear that
Pakistan will likely spend the rest of the year (like India) in a disheveled state. Worse yet? Much
like the last 2 years and especially 2023 have turned out, where a meager 16 vessels were
delivered locally over the entire year, to snap that figure into perspective, this is the number of
vessels across all anchorages just this week.

MARKET SUMMARY
INDIA
The market has firmed this week, with local steel sentiment showing a modest
improvement.
India-U.S. trade discussions are progressing, helping ease currency-related concerns.
Expectations for a stronger INR are improving, offering better liquidity for recyclers
already committed to purchases.
Overall sentiment is more positive than in the past two weeks. Buyers remain cautious,
but firm interest, especially for mid-sized vessels, indicates a healthier buying appetite.
BANGLADESH
Market conditions in Bangladesh remain subdued, with only a small number of
financially capable buyers still active and willing to commit to new tonnage. Demand
for smaller units is minimal, and although some appetite persists for mid- to large-sized
vessels, overall purchasing activity remains soft and selective.
Broader economic challenges across the country continue to weigh heavily on steel
consumption, limiting the ability of local mills to absorb additional tonnage. Even
during the winter season, which is traditionally associated with stronger steel demand,
mills and end users have shown no significant increase in consumption, highlighting
ongoing market weakness.
More mills are shifting to cheaper sponge iron imports from India, reducing reliance on
recycled steel.
PAKISTAN
The Pakistani market shows little change from previous weeks, with sentiment
remaining subdued and most buyers hesitant to engage due to the lack of direction.
Conditions have been stagnant for several weeks, and there are currently no strong
indicators pointing to a near-term shift in demand or momentum.

TURKIYE
As noted in previous updates, the Turkish market continues to show no change, with
conditions remaining exactly the same as in past weeks and no movement or fresh
activity observed.

This has been a disastrous week for nearly all the key indicators tracked in this newsletter, including the state of oil futures,freight rates, local steel plate prices, and disastrous currency devaluations (especially in India). To start, oil found itself slipping rather shockingly as it fell to USD 57.68/barrel - a total drop of over 6% in the last month and over 16% lower than the same time last year. As oil slipped, freight rates continued to climb throughout the week, recording a near 9% decline over the course of 30 days and over 48% as compared to November 2024.
At the same time, governments in the U.S. continue their sanctions and blacklist wave as EU members too are reportedly preparing the 20th batch of sanctions against Russia that could authorize member-state navies and coast guards to board shadow-fleet vessels suspected of carrying sanctioned cargoes. The proposal also considers granting third-country flag administrations permission to deregister such vessels and further tighten flag-state cooperation to curb evasive shipping activities. Moreover, the U.S. continues to tighten Iran's economic activities and levy further sanctions that have now wrapped a reported 170 ships in the tangle of global blacklists.
All of this has trickled down to the ship-recycling industry, as not only is supply getting increasingly restricted (as evident from the quantum of the week's deliveries at the various sub-continent waterfronts), but steel plate prices also fell in both Bangladesh and Pakistan, all while Bangladeshi and (especially) Indian currencies staged a late-Thanksgiving horror show this week. As the trading tables went comparatively quiet, much of the industry remained distracted as many gathered in Hong Kong for the annual Tradewinds Ship Recycling Forum. Among the many topics that were hot points of debate were future trends, supply, HKC developments, the dark-fleet problems plaguing the industry, and what possible solutions (if any) lie ahead. It is apparent that what happens with the dark/shadow fleet is of utmost importance given that over 900 vessels are now on the list with no viable exit or strategy for when they come of recyclable age.
Unlike forums of yesteryear, where plenty of trading and recycling deals were concluded on the conference floor, this year was far more muted in terms of deal flow, supply of vessels, and the dampened pricing and demand. The event was well attended by end users, cash buyers, insurers,

apparent that what happens with the dark/shadow fleet is of utmost importance given that over 900 vessels are now on the list with no viable exit or strategy for when they come of recyclable age.
Unlike forums of yesteryear, where plenty of trading and recycling deals were concluded on the conference floor, this year was far more muted in terms of deal flow, supply of vessels, and the dampened pricing and demand. The event was well attended by end users, cash buyers, insurers, and lawyers, all expecting (more like hoping, after the performance of the last few years) that vessels across the whole trading spectrum that are approaching 30 years of age and older should see a busy few years of activity in 2026.
Finally, there was some encouraging news with the first Pakistani ship-recycling yard set to receive HKC approval reportedly as early as next week, with another 2-3 set to follow in the next three months, while others are expected by the mid-way point of next year. Bangladesh also reported further yards being certified as the number gradually approaches 20. And Turkey? Your guess is as good as ours.

WENTY UP!
Bangladeshi pricing and demand continue to lead the way across global ship recycling markets,
An orexia
even though there have been very few workable candidates to placate increasingly hungry end
users. Although a series of older tankers managed to find their way to end buyers at decent levels
over recent weeks and larger LNGs have remained the flavor of late, it has been a far quieter week
at the waterfront, much more than many had been expecting, and frustrating given the fact that by
this point of the year, the volume of units delivered has been abysmal as the nation continues
battling economic anorexia for much of the year.
With inflation yoyoing around the 8%-9% mark for much of the year and the Bangladeshi Taka
(like India) struggling to stabilize itself since June 2024, this week saw the Taka declining over 20
Some units sold.
basis points to close the week out at BDT 122.53 against the U.S. Dollar - gradually approaching
BDT 123. Local steel plate prices also seem to have hit an oil patch on the road and started declining
over recent weeks, including this week, where they fell again, albeit by a marginal USD 1/Ton, as
they closed the week out at USD 525.93/Ton.
Clearly, the unsold volumes of recycled steel that have been piling up at local recycling yards since
the summer months have left Chattogram chop shops

Clearly, the unsold volumes of recycled steel that have been piling up at local recycling yards since

20 H KC.
cheaper imported scrap steel continues to take the limelight. And with the upcoming elections of

of political violence sprinkling the national headlines.
Meanwhile, another HKC approval has come through this week as the remaining unapproved
yards endure infrastructure upgrades, taking the total to 20 approved HKC yards by various bodies
(NK, BV, IRS, etc.), and several more are set to follow in the month ahead before year end. In fact,
GMS is pleased to report that its Sustainable Ship and Offshore Recycling Program (SSORP) has
certainly helped, as free safety and training sessions for workers across India, Bangladesh, and
SSO RP in action.
Pakistan have been g oing on at pace. As of October 2025, the program successfully completed 766
training sessions and reached 11,730 workers on 69 safety and environmental topics.
Overall, it seems 2025 is destined to end just like 2024 - hammered by availability and collapsing
prices but with an HKC medal in Chattogram's back pocket.

time the vessel(s) arrive(s) and are eventually recycled. The nation's overall economic state too is
slipping into stagflation territory, as inflation fell to just 0.25% through October - down from 1.44% in

PAKISTAN
M
AKEOVER!
Landmark and encouraging news has emerged from Pakistan this week, with the first domestic
Landmark news! ship recycling yard set to be HKC-approved and due for an announcement in the coming week(s)
after all audits are signed off and HKC infrastructure upgrade works are reportedly completed.
This is certainly revelatory news for a market that led the sub-continent price rankings for a healthy
few months in the summer, but has unfortunately fallen behind its sub-continent counterparts ever
since the Hong Kong Convention finally came into force on June 26th, 2025.
Buyers ready to acquire While several more yards are set to follow with HKC approvals within the next 3-6 months to keep
the local momentum moving, another handful are expected to join the fray by the summer of next
year as the industry in Pakistan gets the makeover it has been urging for many a year. Yet, this
promising news will unlikely deviate any tonnage towards Gadani shores on the back of
Mexican stand-off
provisional DASR certifications and approvals.
Moreover, adding to the local negative temperament are the ongoing inflictions from last week
that continued into this week as well, with weakening fundamentals and competing (cheaper) steel
imports from Iran dragging local steel plate prices down by the week - including this one, where
they fell a massive USD 11/Ton to USD 586/Ton - even though they remain the highest in the sub-
continent ship recycling industry. The Pakistani Rupee somehow managed to evade a Mexican
stand-off against the U.S. Dollar as it recovered nearly 5 basis points and ended the week at a still-
high PKR 282.58.
Mo mentum shifting. This is also the third week of no fresh arrivals at the waterfront, with the only local delivery being
that of a distraught vessel that had been idling for over a month at Gadani's waterfront. And like
India, Pakistani recyclers are watching India's pricing movements closely to determine where their
next pricing index will land for the coming week(s), resulting in another no-sale week for this

week at a still-
high PKR 282.58.
Mo mentum shifting. This is also the third week of no fresh arrivals at the waterfront, with the only local delivery being
that of a distraught vessel that had been idling for over a month at Gadani's waterfront. And like
India, Pakistani recyclers are watching India's pricing movements closely to determine where their
next pricing index will land for the coming week(s), resulting in another no-sale week for this
market where the Gadani Gang also refrained from offering.
With the overall macro situation on the freight index not helping ship recycling by way of a
sustained supply, it is likely Gadani will traverse December much like Alang - waiting for some
stability to rein in the maddening state of affairs.

MARKET SUMMARY
INDIA
The Indian market remains cautious this week as persistent and unpredictable dollar
fluctuations continue to affect local sentiment, keeping buyers hesitant.
Purchasing interest for new units, particularly larger vessels, remains limited, with
negotiations becoming increasingly difficult under current currency volatility.
Two new sales appear to have been concluded; however, most of the tonnage being
circulated still consists of sanctioned or "dark fleet" vessels.
Overall, buyers continue to proceed carefully, preferring further clarity on the dollar
before committing to additional tonnage.
BANGLADESH
Market conditions have weakened sharply, with local buyers remaining extremely
cautious amid tightening liquidity in the money market.
Several newly built green-recycling yards have halted operations due to the absence of
incoming vessels, while many existing compliant facilities remain down or fully
occupied.
A notable gap persists between melting scrap and plate prices, further limiting yard
competitiveness and keeping overall buying activity subdued.
Mid- and large-sized vessels still attract some degree of interest, and owners seeking
higher price levels-particularly compared to India, are encouraged to consider
Bangladesh as a viable recycling option.
PAKISTAN
Market sentiment remains stable, though buying interest continues at lower levels as
dollar-related challenges persist.
Pakistan has finally achieved a major milestone with Prime Green Recyclers becoming
the first yard in the country to obtain full HKC compliance, verified by Bureau Veritas.

[Page 3]
Despite this positive development, payment delays of around three to four days
remain common, keeping overall activity cautious. While the certification has boosted
long-term confidence, near-term momentum remains limited.
TURKIYE

TURKIYE
The Turkish market remains stable and unchanged, with no notable movement in
pricing or buying activity.
Conditions mirror previous weeks, with sentiment and demand maintaining a steady but
muted pace. No new indicators suggest any immediate shift in direction.

Global market chaos continues post-Thanksgiving like a bad stomach after too much turkey. Steel plate prices were thehighlight of the entire sub-continent-and even China-where they unanimously retreated in unison this week, while the U.S. Dollar also decided to join in on the fun as it too retreated at all sub-continent locations (except-no surprise-Turkey!). Making matters worse are the rising freight markets that continue to surprise the industry, as the Baltic Exchange's Dry Index climbed another 3.2% through the course of the week, hitting a new high since December 2023 as practically every segment assisted in with the rising rates. Oil, meanwhile, remains at 21st-century lows, idling with marginal fluctuations, yet it closed the week out at USD 59.16/Ton-a near 14% drop over the last 12 months.
As supply continues to be restricted by the widening net of sanctions and blacklists in addition to rising freight rates, it was still a kind gesture by the gods of tonnage that both Bangladesh and India mercifully reported fresh arrivals and deliveries at the waterfront, all while Pakistan's dithering local anchorage returned zilch this week. Prices too remain fragile amidst dreadful fundamentals that haven't gotten a chance to get off their respective trampolines, as most of the tonnage introduced to the markets is either an off-market questionably sourced unit or smaller LDT units that are regularly seeing sub-USD 400/Ton from key locations, creating a dual-priced reality for the markets.
Yet in the midst of uncertainty, a momentous development unraveled this week with the first Pakistani ship-recycling yard to be awarded HKC compliance, as Prime Green Recyclers became the first in the nation to join the array of international yards-bringing Pakistan into the new league. Prime Recyclers attained Bureau Veritas (BV) blessings after the requisite investment and infrastructure upgrades spanning this past year bore fruit, and the matter became critical after the Hong Kong Convention came into force on June 26th. This follows on the heels of 21 Bangladeshi yards receiving their HKC accreditations and a 22nd yard set to reportedly follow imminently, as compliance is firmly underway at both locations to revolutionize and improve domestic standards and catch up to Alang, where over 100 yards have been HKC compliant-some practically for over a decade now.
Overall, as market fundamentals remain confidently uncertain

matter became critical after the Hong Kong Convention came into force on June 26th. This follows on the heels of 21 Bangladeshi yards receiving their HKC accreditations and a 22nd yard set to reportedly follow imminently, as compliance is firmly underway at both locations to revolutionize and improve domestic standards and catch up to Alang, where over 100 yards have been HKC compliant-some practically for over a decade now.
Overall, as market fundamentals remain confidently uncertain, prices have endured a turbulent ride across the board over the last few months. USD 400/LDT+ remains a struggle to achieve in some markets as steel and currencies continue to underperform, alongside the deadly dearth of candidates that has plagued markets for about four years now, with only a handful of sales taking place to increasingly hungry recyclers-particularly in a resurgent Bangladesh, which led the way for sub-continent pricing for another week. Another four weeks and another trip around the sun means the industry continues hoping for a market resurgence in 2026. Tough year!

BANGLADESH
I
N VOGUE!
The focus in Bangladesh over the last five months has been clearly targeted at bringing domestic
HK C focus. ship-recycling yards up to speed, with tremendous progress having been made on HKC
infrastructure upgrade work. Twenty-one yards are now approved (under NK, IR, BV classes), and
another yard has reportedly reached completion, taking the total to 22 set to follow shortly. While
this is certainly encouraging news for the industry as a whole, on an individual basis the fractured
state of supply continues to make ship recyclers at these yards wonder if such gratuitous expenses
during a four-year spell of tonnage drought have been financially worth it, because at the end of
the day, it's all dollars and cents.
And deserved ly so . The last four years have brought massive instability to the nation via
Dollars and Cents skyrocketing and volatile inflation, a Bangladeshi Taka that has dragged local pricing down-
collapsing from the highs of USD 600/Ton to USD 400/Ton since January 2024-political instability,
national riots, uncontrollable monsoons, cheaper steel imports that have, for the first time,
hammered local sentiments in unexpected ways, and local steel plate prices that are escaping down
a chute like a bad dinner.
Speaking of local steel plate prices, they fell even further this week (as they have for several weeks
now), dropping USD 11/Ton and closing the week at USD 506/Ton-a massive fall from above USD
600/Ton and now potentially approaching sub-USD 500/Ton

PAKISTAN
F
IRST HKC!
This has truly been one of the most momentous weeks not only for Pakistan's ship-recycling sector,
Big news! but also for the nation's ship-recycling future, as Prime Green Recyclers of Gadani have become
the first HKC-approved facility in Pakistan, with BV announcing the momentous news this week.
This is a major milestone for the sector as it finally brings Gadani into the big leagues, and all it
will take now is a willing group of shipowners and/or cash buyers to propose vessels locally and
evaluate the efficacy of the upgrades and ensuing recycling processes. As several more yards are
Buyers ready to acquire also undergoing HKC upgrades, it is likely we may see the next round of approvals come through
reportedly within the next 3-4 months.
Yet, like India, the nation's economy remains in a fragile state, as not only did local steel plate
Others to follow.
prices fall USD 7/Ton and wrap the week up at USD 579/Ton, but the Pakistani Rupee firmed a
relatively minor 9 basis points and ended the week at PKR 282. While these numbers are certainly
far better than those from competing India (helping the Gadani Gang maintain their position in the
market rankings), the lack of HKC yards has punished Gadani's anchorage by ending another
week with an utterly empty waterfront.
As such, it has remained a quiet week in terms of sales and activity, and with the bulk of the
tonnage delivered into Bangladesh clearly opening up in the Far East and India taking in units, the
future for Gadani across December will likely maintain the status quo.

MARKET SUMMARY
INDIA
The market remains highly cautious, as the sharp decline in the dollar over the past
two weeks has distorted price expectations, leaving buyers unable to match offered
levels with prevailing local steel prices.
Ship availability is extremely limited, with most of the few units available, heading
toward Bangladesh, resulting in Indian yards staying largely idle.
Current activity is driven primarily by dark-fleet tonnage; however, only a small
number of yards are engaging in such purchases, keeping overall buying interest
subdued.
Although seasonal winter demand is supporting domestic steel consumption,
persistent pressure on steel prices continues to limit any meaningful upside for the
local market.
BANGLADESH
Local market conditions have deteriorated further over the past week, with price levels
continuing to slide and approaching those seen in India.
Steel demand has fallen to multi-year lows, driven by a slowdown in construction
activity and an absence of new government-led infrastructure initiatives.
Market sentiment remains clouded by uncertainty, discouraging buyers from taking
firm positions despite the softer pricing environment.
Expectations are growing that once the current pockets of buying activity conclude,
prices may fall further and eventually align more closely with Indian market levels.
PAKISTAN
Market sentiment has shown slight improvement, supported by recent activity on
smaller units at modest price levels, indicating buyers' willingness to re-engage under
more realistic market conditions.

[Page 3]
Expectations persist that additional tonnage will surface at similar levels, which could
help maintain the market's cautiously positive outlook.
TURKIYE
Market conditions have shown slight improvement, with both import and local dynamics
strengthening, encouraging buyers to improve their offering levels.
With this gradual change, buyers are expecting

As we head into the traditionally quieter festive / Christmas season, ship recycling markets appear to be hamstrungon the back of a stubborn and persistent lack of meaningful tonnage, violently collapsing fundamentals that were promptly followed by declining offers, and all of this ongoing non-stop for nearly all of 2025 that has seen purchasing sentiment in a post-HKC world get crushed under the weight of it all.
To start, the onset of December brought with it a declining Baltic Exchange that saw the Dry Index fall by about 3%, this week, dragged down by mounting pressure across all segments that saw capes slipping 4.4%, panamax dropping 1.4%, and the smaller segments shedding 5 basis points, all while oil barely moved the needle as it closed another week at region USD 59.70/barrel (expected to breach USD 60/barrel in the coming week) and inflation reported minor improvements at a couple of key ship recycling destinations.
But it were financial currencies that really battered both India and Bangladesh, all while Turkey perennially decorated the top three of the declining currency marathon that it alone has been on. Not wanting to be left behind, local steel plate prices also fell by just under USD 5/ton across all sub-continent recycling destinations, leaving ship recyclers firmly holding off on further negotiations as the week ended.
As such, the lack of tonnage has delivered yet another characteristically low supply of vessels for a few weeks now, especially considering the lack of the occasional large LDT LNG vessels (with around 15 sold so far this year) drying up as freight rates continue to remain at elevated overall levels, thus depriving ship recyclers for yet another week. Further cementing this fact was the sheer lack of arrivals at every sub-continent ship recycling location - probably a true first in its own right.
Thankfully, compulsion has successfully driven remarkable developments across the Indian sub-continent ship recycling sector, with HKC infrastructure upgrades still ongoing and a confirmed 23 HKC-certified ship recycling yards now added to the existing sub-continent fleet of HKC certified yards - including Pakistan's first HKC approval that was announced just last week. Ever since the Hong Kong Convention came into force on June 26th earlier in the year, it has been encouraging to see increased investments and upgrades taking place at these new locations, ensuring a better and safer set of standards for responsible recycling.
Overall, 2025 will probably be the cherry-year of the

Ever since the Hong Kong Convention came into force on June 26th earlier in the year, it has been encouraging to see increased investments and upgrades taking place at these new locations, ensuring a better and safer set of standards for responsible recycling.
Overall, 2025 will probably be the cherry-year of the last four and 2026 hopefully brings more tonnage and stability across the globe.

Indian Rupee depreciated against the U.S. Dollar once again by a massive 70 basis points and clock the
week out at a deathly Rs. 89.96, but local steel plate prices also retreated a milder USD 4/Ton and end
the week at nearly USD 387/Ton - clearly highlighting the upcoming levels of local offerings in the
coming weeks.
Unlike their competing neighbours, most Alang yards have been HKC-approved for nearly a decade
now, but the state of fundamentals has driven prices so low that local recyclers have simply been
unable to hammer home the HKC advantage while competing markets are still having their yards
up graded and infrastructure brought up to HKC standards. Notwithstanding, Alang yards have been No advantage.

PAKISTAN

AILGATING!
Prime Green Recyclers has become the first ever HKC-approved ship recycling yard not only in
Primetime! Gadani, but also the first facility to bring Pakistan into the international playground within 2025.
And while progress reportedly continues locally at pace along with infrastructure upgrades, the
rest of the sector remains in shambles as prices have largely tailgated India's (no surprise there),
even though they remain higher on the market rankings.
But the double whammy brought on by both an overall scarcity of tonnage along with a prevailing
Buyers ready to acquire scarcity of HKC-approved yards has left Gadani's anchorage empty once again this week - and
that too for nearly a month now.
After a brief spurt of activity in September backed by comparatively decent levels of DASR assisted
Brief spurt.
local deliveries, local fixtures have all but flatlined over recent weeks as end buyers continue their
HKC development work and take stock of where the market may be heading next.
And rightly so, given that local steel plate prices continue to slip by the week as they fell nearly
USD 5/Ton again and drifted further down to USD 575/Ton (from the highs of USD 650/Ton), as
cheap Iranian imports continue to influence prevailing resale prices of recycled stock. Furthermore,
the Pakistani Rupee miraculously firmed despite its depressed overall state as it gained an
impressive 45 basis points against the U.S. Dollar and end the week at PKR 280.42.
Overall, a lot of work still lies ahead for Pakistan's ship-recycling sector and

 Gamma-ray bursts release more energy in 10 seconds tha n our Sun in its entire life. Nothing in the Universe rivals the
power unleashed during a gamma-ray burst, a brief but incredibly intense flash of high-energy radiation. There are many types
of gamma-ray burst. Some are thought to form when a massive star implodes; others when two neutron stars merge together.
 There are stars we will never be able to see. Ever since the Big Bang, most objects in space have been moving away from
one another. In fact, the expansion of the Universe is actually accelerating. This is one of the facts about space that has really
rocked the cosmological boat over the past few years, and it's known as dark energy. As regions of space are whizzing away
from one another at an ever-increasing rate, the first population of stars to form in the Universe are now too far away for us to
ever hope of spying them. And that's even using the best present or future telescope. Hope is not lost. We can attempt to spot
them indirectly via the energetic bursts of radiation they emit at the end of their lives.

MARKET SUMMARY
INDIA
The market softened during the week, with values continuing to face downward
pressure amid weak overall sentiment.
Ongoing currency weakness continued to weigh on buyer confidence, limiting
participation in the local market.
Buyer participation remained limited throughout the week, as most buyers adopted a
cautious and selective approach.
Overall activity levels stayed subdued, with little evidence of near-term improvement.
BANGLADESH
The local market remained weak during the week, with overall activity levels staying
very low.
Buyer participation continued to be extremely limited, with only one notable Bulker
sale concluded.
The Bulker transaction was achieved at a surprisingly strong level, outperforming
regional alternatives.
Despite weak sentiment and limited buyers, this sale highlights that Bangladesh can
still deliver stronger outcomes for owners seeking higher levels.
PAKISTAN
Market conditions remained unchanged during the week, closely mirroring the
previous period.
Sentiment stayed steady, with no visible shift in buyer behavior.
Overall conditions reflected a market in a wait-and-see mode.

Markets across the board seem to be getting hit by the tragedies of 2025 as December firmly stamps its footprints acrossthe 2025 ship-recycling timeline, ensuring the situation doesn't get a chance to improve just yet! Inflation is on the rise again, plate prices are collapsing in the wrong places, all while the U.S. Dollar continues to rummage through the wreckage of 2025 for whatever it can suck out of ship-recycling currencies and both freight rates & oil futures tumble in unison - just within the span of week 50.
To start, the Baltic Exchange Dry Index reportedly fell nearly 4%, down to its lowest level in nearly a month, dragged by capes that fell 5.6%. Panamax followed suit with a 2.1% drop, and smaller vessels wrapped up the cold blanket with a 16-basis-point drop as the cherry on top. And, surprising many industry traders, oil futures failed to hit the much-anticipated USD 60/barrel mark, instead retreating over 3% to USD 57.61/barrel as the week clocked out - amidst global fears primarily brought on by forecasts predicting an overproduction of oil. The International Energy Agency reaffirming its forecast for a "record supply glut."
Local steel plate prices seemed to slip on the spilling oil figures as they, too, followed suit with noteworthy drops of their own, compounded by slipping currencies as the U.S. Dollar continues to rattle major trading economies.
As the occasional introduction of a market vessel remains the carrot dangling at the end of a drying supply stick, Indian sub-continent ship-recycling markets were already declining, ensuring that the upcoming festive time may perhaps be the perfect moment to reflect and reset after an altogether inadequate and underwhelming few years of horrid supply and ever-declining levels. USD 600/ton was breached back in January 2024, and for a great majority-standing at the doorstep of 2026, and across all sub-continent locations-prices have dipped below the USD 400/ton mark.
While several green vessels have been sold cheaply into Bangladesh over recent weeks, prices here seem to have slipped off the back of a spate of recent deliveries pulling local levels down. Domestic turbulence is unfortunately on the rise in the small nation once again as the final election dates get announced. And although a healthy majority of the recent supply was squarely focused on a Bangladesh redelivery that has

2026, and across all sub-continent locations-prices have dipped below the USD 400/ton mark.
While several green vessels have been sold cheaply into Bangladesh over recent weeks, prices here seem to have slipped off the back of a spate of recent deliveries pulling local levels down. Domestic turbulence is unfortunately on the rise in the small nation once again as the final election dates get announced. And although a healthy majority of the recent supply was squarely focused on a Bangladesh redelivery that has now slipped into the background-joining Indian and Pakistani recycling markets on their downward decline once again-evidence was across all the waterfronts this week, including a surprise.
Finally, HKC developments are ongoing in both Bangladesh and Pakistan, with another three yards expected to receive their HKC accreditations in Chattogram in the new year, while the next batch of yards in Pakistan are expected to follow within Q1 2026 - what a year in recycling it's truly been.
