ERT!
Despite the recent announcement that Prime Green Recyclers became Pakistan's first HKC-ratified
Non serious. ship recycling yard, it has been an absolutely inert time for new (any?) business in Gadani as local
recycling yards struggle to offer anywhere near acceptable levels for any of the sporadic candidates
being offered locally. As a result, anything proposed at the bidding tables almost never greets the
local waterfront, given this market's shortage of HKC yards and prevailing DASR approvals
introducing their own set of hula-hoops to wiggle around in.
Buyers ready to acquire Surprisingly, however, Gadani ended up being the donor of the surprise anchorage of the week,
as-similar to all of its regional competition-local recyclers welcomed at least one smallish LDT
unit that likely opened up in regional waters and decidedly wound up in Pakistan, as it would
have received lower levels from India, along with a longer voyage and more expensive inward
Vessels diverted.
clearance to boot.
Fundamentals here, just like the overall state of global affairs, continue to decimate the ship
recycling landscape. Even though an already high inflation improved slightly to 6.1% from 6.2%
back in October, local steel plate prices (as in India) have been the deadweight dragging sentiments
down of late. After weeks of relentless declines as the market continued to compete against cheaper
Iranian imports, levels flatlined at a still-low USD 575/ton. The Pakistani Rupee too found stable
footing as ended the week where it started, at PKR 280.35 against the U.S. Dollar.
As vigilance on ship recycling gains increasing global attention and Pakistan suffers its unfortunate
Ira nian imports. fate of lagging behind on the HKC spectrum-and as shipowners worldwide grow increasingly
cognizant of their final recycling destinations-even an increase in tonnage supply in 2026 might
not alleviate Pakistan's depressed state.
Another slow year ahead for this market?

MARKET SUMMARY
INDIA
The market showed a marginal improvement during the week, though buying activity
remained slow as fast USD/INR movements continued to affect sentiment and limit
pricing confidence.
Developments surrounding Venezuela added to uncertainty, as tighter enforcement
on sanctioned oil movements raised questions over tanker demand, charter rates, and
the potential impact on global tonnage availability.
With limited clarity on how these factors may evolve, overall sentiment remained
cautious and the market remained in a wait-and-watch mode.
BANGLADESH
The market remained weak during the week, with limited buyer participation amid a
lack of local demand.
Political conditions continued to weigh on sentiment, creating uncertainty and delaying
fresh buying decisions.
While the market is under pressure, it remains too early to confirm any price
correction, and participants are maintaining a wait-and-watch approach.
PAKISTAN
Ship recycling prices remained largely unchanged during the week, with the market
continuing to move in line with previous levels and without any meaningful shift in
sentiment.
Buying interest and activity remained limited, offering little indication of momentum or
near-term improvement.
In the absence of new drivers, the market continued to lack direction, maintaining a flat
and subdued tone.

[Page 3]
TURKIYE
Import prices softened slightly during the week, while local pricing levels remained
largely unchanged.
Despite some movement on the import side, overall sentiment showed no material shift,
with market participants maintaining existing positions.
Activity levels continued without notable change, and the market remained subdued,
reflecting its typically low-volatility nature.

This year doesn't cease to surprise, as December just showed what true volatility can be - in a mere two consecutive weeks at that.What a week this has certainly been, where freight rates continue to fall, the U.S. Dollar forgets which way is up, oil rises yet remains low, local steel plate prices slip oddly across the board at the various destinations, all while Turkey enjoys a forced retirement from the sidelines, watching its currency values fall ever deeper into the abyss.
To start, inflation figures that were never released in the U.S. for October due to the government shutdown did come out for November. In Trump's "hottest country in the world," his regime reported inflation back down to 2.7% last month. India and Bangladesh were both up, but share the same reasons to celebrate, and Turkey was mercifully down as well. The Baltic Exchange Dry Index reported an overall drop of 2.3% this week, brought on by capes, panamax, and smaller vessels collectively dragging the Baltic down by 1.4%, nearly 5%, and 36 basis points, respectively.
This could likely be brought on by the mixed signals out of the U.S. economic inflation figures, along with oil futures that barely rose 73 basis points and close the week out at a globally welcome low of USD 56.86/barrel. As freight rates continue to fall, a collection of market and private fixtures started to announce their arrivals across sub-continent beaches as famished recyclers await upcoming deliveries. And the U.S. Dollar? I'll let the rest of the report speak for itself.
Meanwhile, with Christmas just around the corner, we can reflect on what a disastrous yet pivotal year of upgrades and change across the Indian sub-continent ship recycling markets (and even the ship recycling industry writ large) it has been, with the Hong Kong Convention for safe and responsible recycling finally coming into force on June 26 this year. With this landmark set of initiatives and guidelines finally in force, it is encouraging to see most of the previously 100+ yards HKC-certified by various classification bodies in Alang, 22 yards now in Bangladesh, and only one in Gadani (the rest clearly have some catching up to do) fully operational to a set of globally approved standards.
And just like the previous year, we have seen even lower levels of supply this year, following a 2024 that saw decade-low levels of supply. Although the

of initiatives and guidelines finally in force, it is encouraging to see most of the previously 100+ yards HKC-certified by various classification bodies in Alang, 22 yards now in Bangladesh, and only one in Gadani (the rest clearly have some catching up to do) fully operational to a set of globally approved standards.
And just like the previous year, we have seen even lower levels of supply this year, following a 2024 that saw decade-low levels of supply. Although the performance of freight markets across all sectors has been largely responsible for the ongoing starvation in the supply of tonnage, could recent declines indicate a positive change for the ship recycling industry come 2026? Perhaps the timing in the shortage of supply was apt, as HKC upgrades and adapting to the new rules and regulations - not only for recyclers, but even local authorities to come to grips with - certainly helped everyone.
For 2026, as the average age of a trading vessel climbs from 25 years to 30 years in some cases, and '90s-built bulkers, tankers (albeit in the shadow fleet), and containers aplenty are still trading - and due to be phased out to accommodate newbuilding vessels hitting the water next year - this should hopefully serve up a buffet for the industry. Fingers crossed, Santa!

ILD YEAR!
While some might see that Bangladesh has endured an positive overall year where ship recycling
22 yards approved.
is concerned - given the progress toward HKC-certified recycling, with 22 domestic ship recycling
yards now HKC-accredited by various international bodies and work continuing in the
background on a couple more yards - and given that it has enjoyed more time atop the market
rankings than its regional neighbors for a majority of the year, offering regionally opening tonnage
that extra USD/ton in the bank for all parties involved, that's about where the parade of pride
would likely end.
On the flip side? Literally everything has declined in Bangladesh in 2025. To start, after breaching
the USD 600/t on ma rk back in January 2024, local offerings have now declined by almost USD
Price decline.
200/LDT down to the low USD 400s/LDT, and certainly lower now that a recent surge of tonnage
has local bellies full for the time being. Tariffs, the taka, steel plate prices, and a dearth in the supply
of tonnage has left this market frustrated and deprived of any regular flow of business sense amid
exasperating yard upgrades and infrastructure work, thanks to unexpectedly firm freight trading
rates across the board.
And this week was probably 2025's cherry for Bangladeshi recyclers, as not only did the U.S. Dollar

truly stole the spotlight, plummeting a massive USD 12/ton in just five days and closing the week

makes this an instrumental decline behind the ongoing strength of local offerings. And amid the
HKC celebrations, we lost track of the fact that Bangladesh has now gone from a market of about
35 ship recycling yards to only 22 (even though a few more are reportedly still in the plans).
For the nation at large, it's been even worse: 30% tariffs from Trump; the interim government doing
its best to redefine lethargy; a monsoon that betrayed local yard-upgrade operations; interim

upcoming elections should hopefully jolt the nation's economy back into gear, just in time for the
pre-monsoon shopping bonanza to take on a new meaning.

Encouraging. encouraging to see the first ship recycling yard gain HKC certification last month, but with more
reportedly set to join the ranks within Q1 2026 and 2026 hopefully finally shaping up to be a
healthier year in terms of supply, Gadani has certainly lined itself up to put a serious dent in the
global ship recycling post-HKC era, where redistribution of recyclable tonnage is concerned.
Unfortunately, given Pakistan's ongoing and serious HKC handicap and despite being placed 2nd

it is no surprise that the industry remains wary of a Pakistan redelivery.
Back to buying.

Buyers ready to acquire on the market rankings (in terms of pricing), DASR approvals have done their utmost to ensure
regular business remains a nightmare. With incoming tonnage being regularly subjected to delays,
it is no surprise that the industry remains wary of a Pakistan redelivery.
Back to buying.
Case in point: despite being the only recycling destination in the Indian sub-continent that is not
tide-reliant and has the least restrictive inward requirements, vessels in a post-HKC era seem to be
regularly facing delivery delays, as evidenced by the currently idling mini-VL that has been sitting
at anchorage for nearly 20 days now.
Domestic fundamentals too have notably done more to help than hurt Gadani of late, as local steel
plate prices jumped by over USD 4/ton and closed the week out near USD 580/ton - an industry-
leading margin at present. Moreover, the recently firming PKR - which found itself back down
around PKR 280 (from PKR 283) - lost 13 basis points but still closed the week out at PKR 280.30.
Ne xt approvals due. Overall, as work continues locally and more yards are set to join the global HKC fray, and as long
as fundamentals hold their ground, Gadani is certainly set to have a busy year ahead - especially
for the HKC-certified crew.

in Weekly Demolition Reports 29/12/2025
Image reference: 2025-12-29_gms-week-52-au-revoir-2025_img1.jpg
A turbulent 2025 - not only for the ship recycling industry, but for global economies churning underthe weight of tariffs, tantrums, and tumultuous international situations - finally comes to a close, there were certainly some positive highlights to note as remarkable progress on HKC infrastructure upgrades has been carried out across the entire Indian sub-continent ship recycling industry right before the year-end - a leaving a positive mark for the ship recycling industry and certainly a welcome sign for 2026.
Yet, the glaring fact remains that prices are far poorer than the USD 600/ton highs of January 2024, driven by consistently declining steel plate prices across the Indian sub-continent markets, coupled with incredible volatility in global currencies/foreign exchange trading, never-ending political events, and a touch of monsoon rainstorms that even brought the weather into the mix. As such, markets continue to shift and slide, while the ongoing scarcity in vessel supply remains ever consistent. It has also been a quiet week at the bidding tables (Christmas mood notwithstanding) and another largely uneventful - and eerily quiet - year on the recycling waterfront, punctuated occasionally by the odd large LDT LNG and older bulker.
But that we hope will change going into 2026, as the Baltic Exchange continued reporting downward trajectory for the dry sectors, falling another 0.8% this week to its lowest levels since October. The decline was driven by an overall retreat in capes (down 0.5%) and the smaller segments that shed 18 basis points. Oil on the other hand, managed to climb to USD 57.21/barrel - a 0.82% increase this week - yet it registered an overall 3.56% drop across December, and a 19.2% overall decline compared to the same time last year. Local steel plate prices, too, surprisingly donned new hats this week, shifting from rising, to falling, to flat-out lack of trading.
As 2026 rolls around next week, and with many expecting freight markets to continue performing into the first half of 2026, the introduction of vessels for recycling could therefore be potentially delayed even further - at least until surveys and dry dockings start kicking in. Hopefully, this delay should create the opportunity for

decline compared to the same time last year. Local steel plate prices, too, surprisingly donned new hats this week, shifting from rising, to falling, to flat-out lack of trading.
As 2026 rolls around next week, and with many expecting freight markets to continue performing into the first half of 2026, the introduction of vessels for recycling could therefore be potentially delayed even further - at least until surveys and dry dockings start kicking in. Hopefully, this delay should create the opportunity for both Pakistan and Bangladesh to make meaningful progress on their HKC journeys, as more yards are expected to be approved by then and ready to take on fresh units in 2026, in line with the Hong Kong Convention.
As 2026 looms and a quiet week ensues, we at GMS wish all our readers and friends, a Happy Christmas and a Happy, Healthy, and Prosperous New Year ahead!

In dia remains planted firmly at the foot of the global ship recycling market rankings for yet another
Planted at foot.
week/month, capping off an overall miserable year of declining rates, a frighteningly depreciating

As competing sub-continent markets continue to catch up on the number of HKC-accredited yards in

Missed opportunity.

TARVED PRIDE!
As 2025 draws to a close, Pakistan can only look back with pride as the nation's first HKC-approved
First HKC. ship recycling yard joined the international fray - not only for Pakistan, but as a first step onto
the global stage.
While earlier in the year, it seemed like an impossibility for Pakistan to catch up with HKC yard-
upgrade requirements within 2025 (given how far behind local progress staggered), serious
investments and infrastructure updates have taken place in the interim to speed up the process.
Buyers ready to acquire And with many more yards reportedly on the way scheduled for Q1 2026 approvals, Pakistan may
become the one market capable of putting a serious dent into India's HKC prowess west of
Bangladesh.
Serious work.

that have remained comparatively tame and stronger. The PKR gained four basis points to close a
year that started at PKR 278.

Serious work.

that have remained comparatively tame and stronger. The PKR gained four basis points to close a
year that started at PKR 278.30 against the U.S. Dollar, at PKR 280.13. And despite a USD 80/ton
drop, local steel plate prices remained flatlined through the week at around USD 580/ton (well
ahead of the competition), having started the year near USD 640/ton.
While Gadani buyers did get back to buying with a mid-year spree, thanks to firmer fundamentals
and timing, this was not at sustained throughput as imports of cheap Iranian steel products started
to hammer local sentiment - and local offer levels for tonnage as a result.
Ba ck to buying. Yet, like Chattogram, with inflation now declining last month, relatively stable fundamentals, and
only additions to the number of HKC-accredited ship recycling yards, the only way for the Gadani
Gang in 2026 is up.

[Page 2]
MARKET SUMMARY
INDIA
The local market showed a marginal improvement compared to the previous week,
with sentiment slightly firmer overall.
However, due to adverse currency exchange movements, buyers who purchased over
the last three to four months continue to face losses, keeping confidence restrained.
Buying interest is present, but at a much lower level than previously seen, with buyers
remaining cautious and selective.
Dark fleet vessel arrivals continue to keep the market busy, consistent with the trend
reported in recent weeks. Several yards that were previously empty are now being
filled with these incoming vessels, supporting activity despite broader caution.
BANGLADESH
The market remained largely unchanged during the week, with weak sentiment and
subdued activity as buyers continue to find it difficult to operate under current
conditions.
Buyers remain reluctant to purchase newer assets, especially as prices continue to
remain quite low compared to past levels.
Ongoing severe civil unrest across Bangladesh continues to weigh on overall sentiment
and confidence.
PAKISTAN
The market showed no meaningful change during the week, continuing to remain quiet
and subdued.
Recent activity was limited to one private deal involving a small bulker, offering little
indication of broader momentum.
Expectations remain that Pakistan will focus on smaller tonnage until a larger unit
becomes available at workable prices.

[Page 3]
In the absence of stronger drivers, the market may face downward pressure in the
near term.
TURKIYE
The market remained very slow during the week, with no notable change in sentiment
or pricing direction.
Similar to last week, overall conditions stayed flat, with participants holding existing
positions.
Import and local pricing showed no fresh movement, reflecting continued inactivity
and limited interest.
